name: Judgment Sensitivity Theory
executive_summary:
Judgment Sensitivity Theory (JST) applies the formal apparatus of signal detection theory (SDT) to the problem of human judgment noise documented by Kahneman, Sibony, and Sunstein. SDT, developed in psychophysics and radar engineering, decomposes any detection task into two independent parameters: discriminability (d-prime), which measures how well the observer can distinguish signal from noise, and criterion bias (c), which measures the observer's threshold for saying 'yes.' JST's central claim is that most interventions designed to improve human judgment -- structured decision protocols, checklists, rubrics, calibration training -- work primarily by increasing collective d-prime rather than by correcting bias. This is a counterintuitive reframing: the conventional wisdom assumes that structured protocols reduce bias, but JST argues they reduce noise, which is mathematically equivalent to improving discriminability.

The distinction matters enormously for intervention design. If judgment errors are primarily bias-driven, the correct remedy is debiasing (training people to recognize and correct systematic distortions). If they are primarily noise-driven, the correct remedy is noise reduction: narrowing the distribution of judgments through structural constraints that reduce occasion-to-occasion and judge-to-judge variability. JST predicts that noise reduction interventions will improve accuracy even in contexts where aggregate bias is already near zero -- because even unbiased judges with high noise produce poor decisions on individual cases. This prediction is directly testable via ROC analysis: noise reduction should shift the ROC curve upward (higher d-prime) without necessarily moving the operating point along the curve (unchanged bias).

JST further predicts that the benefits of judge aggregation (wisdom of crowds) follow a sqrt(n) law for d-prime only when judges' noise is uncorrelated. When judges share training, institutional culture, or information sources, their noise becomes correlated and aggregation produces diminishing returns much faster than sqrt(n) predicts. This has direct implications for panel design in law, medicine, and hiring: adding more judges from the same institutional background yields less improvement than adding fewer judges from diverse backgrounds, because diversity decorrelates noise rather than canceling bias.

mapped_components.unit: A single judge's evaluation of a single case, modeled as one SDT trial. The 'signal' is the true merit or quality of the case (guilt, qualification, disease presence). The 'noise' includes all sources of variability: the judge's internal state, the case's presentation, environmental factors, and the judge's idiosyncratic weighting of features.
mapped_components.variation: The noise-only and signal-plus-noise distributions for each judge, widened by three independent noise sources: (1) within-judge occasion noise (the same judge evaluates the same case differently on different days), (2) between-judge noise (different judges evaluate the same case differently), and (3) system noise (the case presentation itself varies in ways uncorrelated with true merit).
mapped_components.selection: Aggregation rules function as criterion-placement strategies. Simple averaging, majority voting, structured deliberation, and algorithmic scoring each place the collective criterion at different points and with different effective noise levels. Structured protocols are selected for because they narrow the noise distributions themselves, not merely because they move the criterion.
mapped_components.fitness: Collective d-prime: the panel's ability to discriminate between truly different cases, measured independently of any directional bias. A high-fitness judgment system produces tight ROC curves with large areas under the curve. Fitness degrades when noise sources become correlated (shared training, groupthink) or when the evaluation task itself has low signal-to-noise ratio.
mapped_components.boundary: The theory assumes: (1) approximately Gaussian noise distributions, (2) stationary signal and noise parameters within a judgment session, (3) judges whose errors are at least partially independent. Violations occur with: extreme cases where all judges agree (no noise to reduce), very low-expertise judges (noise swamps signal entirely), or highly correlated judges (aggregation fails to improve d-prime).

falsifiable_predictions: Implementing structured evaluation protocols (rubrics, checklists, sequential assessment) will improve judgment accuracy even in settings where the mean bias across judges is already near zero. The improvement will be visible as an increase in d-prime in ROC analysis, not as a shift in criterion.

Adding independent judges to a panel will improve collective d-prime following a sqrt(n) scaling law, but only when judges' noise is uncorrelated. Panels of judges from the same institution or training program will show significantly flatter scaling (slower d-prime improvement per additional judge) than panels of judges from diverse backgrounds.

Occasion noise (within-judge variability) and between-judge noise make separable contributions to total judgment error, and they respond to different interventions. Standardized case presentation reduces occasion noise; diverse panel composition reduces the damage of between-judge noise correlation. A 2x2 experiment (standardized vs. unstandardized presentation x homogeneous vs. diverse panel) should show independent main effects.

Higher-expertise judges (higher individual d-prime) will benefit less from aggregation than lower-expertise judges, producing an expertise-by-group-size interaction. This is because high-d-prime judges already have narrow noise distributions, leaving less room for sqrt(n) improvement.

In domains where decision noise has been measured (sentencing, insurance underwriting, medical diagnosis, hiring), the ratio of noise-to-bias in total error will exceed 1.5:1, and interventions targeting noise reduction will produce larger accuracy improvements per unit cost than interventions targeting bias reduction.

Forced ranking protocols (where judges must rank cases rather than rate them independently) will produce higher effective d-prime than independent rating protocols for the same judges evaluating the same cases, because ranking eliminates scale-use noise -- a prediction derivable from SDT but not from bias-focused frameworks.

supporting_evidence.finding: Kahneman, Sibony, and Sunstein (2021) documented pervasive 'noise' in professional judgment across domains including criminal sentencing, insurance underwriting, medical diagnosis, and personnel evaluation. They found that variability between judges (and within the same judge on different occasions) was typically larger than systematic bias, and that organizations systematically underestimate the magnitude of noise in their decisions.
supporting_evidence.relevance: Provides the empirical foundation that JST formalizes. The finding that noise exceeds bias in magnitude across many domains is exactly the condition under which JST's SDT-based framework adds the most value over traditional bias-focused approaches.
supporting_evidence.source: Kahneman, D., Sibony, O., & Sunstein, C.R. (2021). Noise: A Flaw in Human Judgment. Little, Brown Spark.

supporting_evidence.finding: Signal detection theory research in radiology has demonstrated that structured reporting protocols and decision aids improve diagnostic accuracy primarily by increasing d-prime (sensitivity to true abnormalities) rather than by shifting criterion (willingness to call a finding positive). Beam et al. (2003) showed that computer-aided detection improved mammographic sensitivity without changing specificity, consistent with a d-prime improvement rather than a criterion shift.
supporting_evidence.relevance: Validates JST's central mechanism in a high-stakes medical judgment domain. The SDT decomposition shows that structured aids improve discrimination rather than merely changing decision thresholds, exactly as JST predicts for judgment protocols more broadly.
supporting_evidence.source: Beam, C.A., Conant, E.F., & Sickles, E.A. (2003). Association of volume and volume-independent factors with accuracy in screening mammogram interpretation. JNCI, 95(4), 282-290.

supporting_evidence.finding: Research on wisdom-of-crowds effects (Surowiecki, 2004; Mannes et al., 2014) has shown that averaging independent judgments improves accuracy following approximately sqrt(n) laws, but that accuracy gains diminish rapidly when judges share information or deliberate before reporting. This is consistent with noise correlation reducing the effective independence of judges.
supporting_evidence.relevance: Empirically confirms JST's prediction about the role of noise correlation in aggregation. The finding that information sharing degrades crowd wisdom maps directly to JST's claim that correlated noise reduces the d-prime benefit of adding judges.
supporting_evidence.source: Mannes, A.E., Soll, J.B., & Larrick, R.P. (2014). The wisdom of select crowds. Journal of Personality and Social Psychology, 107(2), 276-299.

experimental_designs.name: SDT Decomposition of Judicial Sentencing Noise
experimental_designs.description: Recruit 100 sitting judges to evaluate 50 pre-scored criminal cases (with independently established 'true severity' ratings from expert panels). Each judge evaluates all 50 cases under two conditions (counterbalanced): (a) unstructured judgment (read case file, assign sentence), (b) structured protocol (sequential checklist addressing specific aggravating/mitigating factors, forced severity rating before sentencing). Compute individual and collective d-prime and criterion values for each condition using ROC analysis on the severity ratings versus true severity.
experimental_designs.hypothesis: The structured protocol will increase mean d-prime by 0.3-0.5 standard units without significantly changing mean criterion. Between-judge noise (variance of individual d-prime values) will also decrease under the structured condition. The effect will persist even among judges whose individual bias is near zero.
experimental_designs.feasibility: Moderate-to-difficult. Requires judicial cooperation and IRB approval. The 'true severity' ground truth is the main methodological challenge -- using expert consensus panels with demonstrated reliability as the reference standard is the best available approach. Simulated case files can be used if real case access is restricted.

experimental_designs.name: Noise Correlation and Panel Diversity Experiment
experimental_designs.description: Recruit 300 hiring managers from 30 companies (10 per company). Each evaluates the same set of 40 job candidate profiles. Construct panels of size 3, 5, and 7 using two strategies: (a) same-company panels (judges from the same organization), (b) cross-company panels (judges from different organizations). For each panel size and composition, compute collective d-prime against independently established candidate quality scores (from subsequent job performance data or expert panels).
experimental_designs.hypothesis: Cross-company panels will show steeper d-prime scaling with panel size (closer to sqrt(n)) than same-company panels. At panel size 7, cross-company panels will achieve d-prime values 20-40% higher than same-company panels, despite using judges with equivalent individual d-prime values. The difference will be attributable to lower noise correlation in cross-company panels.
experimental_designs.feasibility: High if conducted within a large multi-company hiring platform or recruiting consortium. The key requirement is access to enough judges and a common evaluation task. Ground truth can be established via longitudinal performance tracking of actually hired candidates, though this extends the study timeline.

limitations: The Gaussian noise assumption underlying SDT may not hold for all judgment domains. In contexts with heavy-tailed error distributions (occasional extreme outlier judgments), the d-prime framework underestimates the impact of rare catastrophic errors and overestimates the benefit of noise-reduction interventions that primarily affect the distribution center.

JST treats noise as something to be eliminated, but some noise may be functional. In evolutionary and organizational contexts, variability in judgment can serve as an exploration mechanism, preventing premature convergence on suboptimal solutions. The theory does not distinguish between harmful noise (inconsistency that reduces accuracy) and beneficial noise (variability that enables adaptation).

The theory requires a well-defined 'signal' -- a true case quality that exists independently of the judgment process. In domains where quality is socially constructed (art evaluation, cultural fit assessment, moral judgment), there may be no objective signal against which to measure d-prime, making the SDT framework formally inapplicable even if intuitively appealing.

JST assumes that bias and noise are separable and independently targetable. In practice, interventions that reduce noise may also shift bias (e.g., a structured protocol might standardize judgments around a biased template), and interventions targeting bias may increase noise (e.g., encouraging judges to reconsider default assumptions may increase within-judge variability). The interaction between noise and bias reduction is underspecified.

The practical recommendations (diverse panels, structured protocols) are well-known and already widely implemented in many domains. JST provides a more rigorous theoretical justification for these practices, but the incremental predictive value over the simpler heuristic 'use structured processes and diverse panels' may be modest in applied settings.

connections_to_other_frameworks.relationship: Sibling framework in the behavioral economics cluster. DTN addresses the temporal dynamics of judgment (when decisions are made affects their quality), while JST addresses the structural decomposition of judgment accuracy. Together they suggest that both the timing and the protocol of judgment affect d-prime.

connections_to_other_frameworks.framework: Zero-Knowledge Proof Theory of Therapeutic Alliance
connections_to_other_frameworks.relationship: Both frameworks address information asymmetry in evaluation contexts. JST models how evaluators extract signal from noise; ZKPT models how trust can be established without revealing the underlying information. They may intersect in contexts like psychiatric evaluation where the signal must be extracted from inherently noisy self-report.

APT addresses how institutional decisions evolve over time; JST addresses how to improve individual decision quality. In legal contexts, JST's noise reduction through structured protocols could be the mechanism by which APT's 'adaptive precedents' converge toward better decisions across generations of cases.

potential_applications: Judicial system reform: Deploy SDT-based audit protocols that decompose sentencing variability into d-prime and criterion components, enabling courts to identify whether disparities are driven by noise (requiring standardization) or bias (requiring debiasing), and target interventions accordingly.

Medical diagnostic panel design: Optimize the composition of tumor boards, diagnostic committees, and peer-review panels by selecting for noise decorrelation (diverse training backgrounds) rather than simple expertise level, potentially improving diagnostic accuracy without increasing panel size or cost.

Hiring process optimization: Redesign interview panels using JST principles -- replace multiple interviews with the same hiring manager (high occasion noise) with structured single interviews by diverse panelists (noise decorrelation), and use forced-ranking protocols to eliminate scale-use noise.

AI-human hybrid judgment: Use JST to determine optimal human-AI panel composition. AI judges have zero occasion noise but potentially systematic bias; human judges have high occasion noise but different bias profiles. JST predicts the optimal ratio based on their respective d-prime and noise correlation structures.

Educational assessment: Redesign grading rubrics and exam scoring protocols based on JST's decomposition, identifying which scoring dimensions contribute most to between-grader noise and targeting those dimensions for structural constraint.
